sections.label: USER PROMPT
sections.content:
The code executed 1 action in the previous sequence.
Executed actions: MOUSE(row=16, col=42).
You are still on the same level.
Current state: step 34, level 2.
Valid actions right now: UP, DOWN, LEFT, RIGHT, MOUSE, ACTION7.
Only tool: `python`. It receives `current_frame`, `previous_frame`, `history`, `transitions`, `last_transition`, `valid_actions`, `last_action_result`, and `action(actions)`. It also provides `last_animation` (per-action frames of your last `action(...)` call) and `frame_stats` (how much this game has animated so far) -- optional; use them only if you judge the intermediate motion informative rather than incidental.
Only letter-coded board views and lightweight metadata are exposed; raw numeric color IDs are not available.
Keep tool output compact: use `current_frame.segmentation` as the primary view, and `current_frame.ascii` only for a small specific region; never print full boards.
For the most recent change, compare `previous_frame` to `current_frame`, or `last_transition.before_frame` to `last_transition.after_frame`; `history[-1].frame` is the current frame, not the previous one.
Use Python to inspect the evidence, refine that world model from the newest history, and search or score candidate actions or short sequences against the current goal as you currently understand it.
Maintain a compact working world model of what the current level seems to contain, what actions appear to do, what the goal seems to be, what is still uncertain, and what plan currently looks best.
Below you are provided with the current world model from the previous turn. The default behavior is to copy it and add or remove things based on the evidence that you gathered. BEFORE EXECUTING NEW ACTIONS YOU MUST ALWAYS GIVE THE REVISED VERSION OF THE WORLD MODEL.
You may call `action(actions)` more than once in one Python snippet if your search or control loop needs it, but stop immediately if a result reports `game_over`, `run_complete`, `level_completed`, or `done`.
Working world model carried from earlier turns:
- World model: Click at (16, 41) had no effect - only the timer changed. The greens remain at (16, 38), (16, 44), (52, 44). The midpoint calculation may be off.
- Goal model: Need to find the correct position between (16, 38) and (16, 44).
- Plan: Check exact boundaries of the green objects to find the proper click position.
- Revise any item above immediately if `current_frame` or `history` contradicts it.
end of world model. 
Focus on what changed most recently in `history`, update the target environment change if needed, and separate gameplay-object changes from HUD-only changes.
When ready, call `action(actions)` from inside the `python` tool with the best valid action or ordered batch selected by your code. If your code has found a reliable short sequence, prefer batching it in one call.
You may call `action(actions)` more than once in one Python snippet if your search or control loop needs it.
If you include assistant text before a tool call, keep it short and use it to update the world model. Helpful optional prefixes are `World model:`, `Goal model:`, `Action model:`, `Recent findings:`, `Open questions:`, `Plan:`, and `Cross-level notes:`.
When calling `python`, emit exactly the tool-call format shown elsewhere in this prompt for this model. Use only that format; do not add markdown fences, prose wrappers, or alternate tool-call syntax. Do not quote or place tool-call markup inside explanatory text; when you decide to call the tool, emit the tool call itself.
If you use MOUSE, include integer row and col arguments.
EFFICIENCY BUDGET — your score on each level is (baseline_actions / your_actions)^2, so every wasted action costs you quadratically.
Level 2: you have used 6 of about 81 target actions so far.

sections.label: THINKING
sections.content:
Interesting! Clicking at (16, 42) selected the green at (16, 44) with G border and placed g marks at a different location - centered around (16, 32). But wait, (16, 32) is to the LEFT of the selected green. This is different from the previous pattern where the g marks were placed to the right.
Looking at the g marks:
- (15, 32)
- (16, 31)
- (16, 34)
- (18, 32)
The center is around (16, 32). But there's no green at (16, 32) anymore - it was consumed. The g marks are pointing to an empty space.
Wait, let me reconsider. The pattern might be different now. Looking at the remaining greens:
- (16, 38)
- (16, 44) - selected with G border
- (52, 44)
The g marks are at (16, 32) which is to the left of (16, 38). This doesn't match the previous pattern. Maybe the game is selecting a different pair now.
Actually, I think the pattern might be: when you click between two greens, it selects the RIGHT one (not the left one as I thought before). Let me re-examine:
- First click at (16, 17) between (16, 14) and (16, 20): selected (16, 14) - the LEFT one
- Second click at (16, 29) between (16, 26) and (16, 32): selected (16, 26) - the LEFT one
- Now clicking at (16, 42) between (16, 38) and (16, 44): selected (16, 44) - the RIGHT one
Hmm, that's inconsistent. Let me think about what's different. The g marks are at (16, 32), which is where a green WAS (it was consumed). Maybe the game is trying to "rewind" or go back?
Actually wait - maybe the g marks are pointing to where the NEXT green should go. In the previous pattern:
- After selecting (16, 14), g marks appeared between (16, 20) and (16, 32) at (16, 26)
- After selecting (16, 26), g marks appeared between (16, 32) and (16, 44) at (16, 38)
- Now after selecting (16, 44), g marks appeared at (16, 32)... but that's not between any two remaining greens
Wait, maybe the g marks are pointing to the position of the green that was consumed. Let me check:
- After selecting (16, 14), g marks at (16, 26) - but (16, 20) was the next green, not consumed yet
- After selecting (16, 26), g marks at (16, 38) - but (16, 32) was the next green
Actually I think I need to reconsider. The g marks appear BETWEEN the next two greens in sequence. But now with only (16, 38), (16, 44), and (52, 44) remaining:
- (16, 44) is selected
- The next two greens would be... (16, 38) and (52, 44)? That doesn't make sense spatially.
Let me just click at the g marks center (16, 32) and see what happens.

sections.label: ASSISTANT
sections.content:
World model: Clicking at (16, 42) selected green at (16, 44) with G border and placed g marks centered around (16, 32). This is different from the pattern - the g marks are to the left of the selected green.
Goal model: Click the g marks to continue the consumption pattern.
Plan: Click at (16, 32) - center of the g marks.

sections.label: TOOL CALL: python
sections.content:
<tool_call>
<function=python>
<parameter=code>
# Click at center of g marks: (16, 32)
result = action([{'action': 'MOUSE', 'row': 16, 'col': 32}])

# Check result
before_ascii = last_transition.before_frame.ascii.split('\n')
after_ascii = last_transition.after_frame.ascii.split('\n')
diff_rows = [i for i in range(len(before_ascii)) if before_ascii[i] != after_ascii[i]]
print(f"Changed rows: {diff_rows}")
for r in diff_rows:
    if r != 0:
        print(f"Row {r}: {before_ascii[r][10:50]} -> {after_ascii[r][10:50]}")

# Check remaining greens and G/g marks
seg = current_frame.segmentation
n_nodes = [n for n in seg['nodes'] if n['color'] == 'N']
g_nodes = [n for n in seg['nodes'] if n['color'] in ('G', 'g')]
print(f"\nRemaining N nodes: {len(n_nodes)}")
for n in n_nodes:
    center = (sum(p[0] for p in n['boundary'])//len(n['boundary']), sum(p[1] for p in n['boundary'])//len(n['boundary']))
    print(f"  center={center}")
print(f"G/g nodes: {len(g_nodes)}")
for n in g_nodes:
    center = (sum(p[0] for p in n['boundary'])//len(n['boundary']), sum(p[1] for p in n['boundary'])//len(n['boundary']))
    print(f"  {n['color']}: center={center}, pixels={n['pixels']}")
</parameter>
</function>
</tool_call>
